retrieved_docs.title: Endomyocardial Fibrosis -- Evaluation -- Echocardiography
retrieved_docs.content: Echocardiography is the primary diagnostic tool for identifying classical presentations of endomyocardial fibrosis. [46] Diagnostic criteria are based on echocardiographic parameters, and endomyocardial fibrosis is confirmed when 2 major or 1 major and 2 minor criteria are met. [31] This modality helps assess the extent and severity of the disease by identifying key pathological features such as apical obliteration and endocardial surface thrombi. Microbubble contrast enhances imaging quality, allowing for better visualization of the left ventricular cavity, atrioventricular valve abnormalities, and restrictive filling patterns.

retrieved_docs.title: Endomyocardial Fibrosis -- Evaluation -- Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), while relatively expensive and not widely accessible, proves valuable for early diagnosis of endomyocardial fibrosis by detecting subendocardial fibrosis with late gadolinium enhancement, often preceding cavity obliteration and restrictive filling patterns. [50] More sensitive than echocardiography in detecting intracardiac thrombi, cardiac MRI is critical in monitoring treatment response and assisting in preoperative assessments. [51] [52] By offering detailed tissue characterization, cardiac MRI has been shown to improve early detection of endomyocardial fibrosis, facilitating timely intervention to improve patient outcomes. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Endomyocardial Fibrosis -- Evaluation -- Echocardiography
retrieved_docs.content: In right-sided endomyocardial fibrosis, obliteration of the trabecular aspect of the right ventricular cavity is noted. [47] With further disease advancement, the right ventricular cavity volume and worsening tricuspid regurgitation are reduced due to tricuspid annular dilatation. Spontaneous echo contrast and right atrial thrombi are also commonly observed. In left-sided endomyocardial fibrosis, ventricular apex obliteration leads to restricted movement and compensatory hypercontractility in the basal ventricle. Fibrosis affecting the posterior mitral valve leaflet causes severe eccentric mitral regurgitation, and both forms of EMF show atrial enlargement. [48] Doppler echocardiography reveals a restrictive filling pattern characterized by a short deceleration time, shortened isovolumetric relaxation time, and rapid early diastolic filling followed by minimal flow in late diastole. Reduced flow propagation velocity has also been documented in many patients. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Endomyocardial Fibrosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Sudden cardiac death: Although less common, the combination of arrhythmias, thromboembolic events, and advanced heart failure can increase the risk of sudden cardiac death.

retrieved_docs.content: Endomyocardial fibrosis leads to a range of complications due to progressive fibrotic changes in the heart, resulting in restrictive cardiomyopathy. Common complications include:

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Rupture -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial rupture often occurs during the breakdown of collagen and the formation of new fibrous tissue. Factors that increase the risk of rupture after an acute MI include a strong inflammatory response, the presence of matrix metalloproteinases, and intramyocardial hemorrhage. [18] After an acute MI, myocytes show changes like cytoplasmic hypereosinophilia and nuclear pyknosis, giving them a distinctive wavy appearance. About 8 hours after the infarction, interstitial edema and neutrophilic infiltration appear. Within 24 hours, the myocytes lose their cross-striations, and focal hyalinization occurs. By 96 hours, the process of degrading and removing necrotic fibers begins. Collagenase activity spikes on day 2 and peaks by day 7, leading to collagen breakdown. New collagen fibers start forming by day 14. After 4 to 6 weeks, the necrotic myocardium is completely removed and replaced by scar tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Viability -- Evaluation -- Contrast-Enhanced Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR)
retrieved_docs.content: The initial method of viability assessment using CMR is quantifying end-diastolic wall thickness (EDWT). A myocardial thickness of less than 6 mm is likely a 'scar' and less likely to improve after revascularization. One of the fundamental histologic changes in necrotic myocardium is extracellular volume expansion and death of myocytes. This change enables the high uptake and retention of high-density gadolinium-based contrast agents in the cell as well as extracellular space, termed late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) and forms the basis for a viability study with contrast-enhanced CMR. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Right Heart Failure -- Evaluation -- Magnetic resonance imaging
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned, MRI is now the gold standard for measuring right ventricle volumes and function. Besides the right ventricle mass, volumes, and chamber dimensions, MRI can quantify regurgitant volumes, delayed enhancement, scar burden, strain, perfusion, and pulmonary pulsatility. Changes in the global function of the right ventricle after medical therapy have also been shown to directly correlate with functional class and survival in patients with PAH. However, MRI is limited by its temporal resolution, susceptibility artifacts in patients with implantable cardiac devices, and the time required for data acquisition and analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocardial Fibroelastosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can be useful in the detection of EFE since biopsy is invasive. A hypointense rim in the perfusion sequence of the myocardium and a hyperintense rim in the delayed-enhancement sequence is indicative of EFE. [29] Biopsy of endocardial tissue is the gold standard test for diagnosis but may not be the first choice due to its invasive nature and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Different imaging techniques are used to assess myocardial perfusion, myocardial viability, myocardial thickness, thickening and motion, and the effect of myocyte loss on the kinetics of para-magnetic or radio-opaque contrast agents indicating myocardial fibrosis or scars. [14] Some imaging modalities that can be used are echocardiography, radionuclide imaging, and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (cardiac MRI). Regional wall motion abnormalities induced by ischemia can be detected by echocardiography almost immediately after the onset of ischemia when greater than 20% transmural myocardial thickness is affected. Cardiac MRI provides an accurate assessment of myocardial structure and function. [14]

These procedures involve surgically accessing the left or right ventricular chamber, respectively, to obtain myocardial tissue samples for diagnostic evaluation. By being both a type of heart biopsy and an integral component of open‑heart surgery, they illustrate how tissue sampling is incorporated into invasive cardiac operative techniques to inform patient management and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy), indicating that structural changes in the left ventricle often accompany abnormal heart rhythms. In addition, cardiac dysrhythmias frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter), reflecting a common overlap of rhythm disturbances that can influence diagnosis, management, and prognosis in patients with cardiac electrical abnormalities. Recognizing the link between cardiac dysrhythmias, left ventricular hypertrophy, and atrial flutter is important for comprehensive cardiac assessment and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: Supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and ventricular tachycardia are sub‑categories of cardiac electrical physiological studies (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية), which include arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and targeted treatment of tachyarrhythmias. Structural heart disease commonly co‑occurs with these rhythm disturbances: left‑ventricular hypertrophy (تضخم البطين الأيسر) and congestive cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني) are linked to atrial fibrillation (الرجفان الأذيني) and supraventricular acceleration; rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي) is frequently present with supraventricular tachycardia. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2236
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY can present with the manifestation of اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني (congestive cardiomyopathy). In turn, اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني is clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left‑ventricular hypertrophy). This linkage indicates that patients with this conduction disorder, whether or not they develop dilated cardiomyopathy, may progress to a congestive form of cardiomyopathy that commonly exhibits left‑ventricular hypertrophy, underscoring the need for careful cardiac monitoring and assessment of ventricular structure in their care.
